serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tea leaf salad is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a composed plate with pickled tea leaves at the centre surrounded by fried legumes, nuts, dried shrimp, and fresh vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Lahpet Thoke (Pickled Tea Leaf Salad) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients: fermented/pickled tea leaves, peanuts, sesame seeds, fried legumes, dried shrimp, tomato, lime juice, fish sauce, and vegetable oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for lahpet thoke as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each major component: pickled tea leaves (approximated from green tea leaf data, USDA NDB 14355 and related), dry-roasted peanuts (USDA FDC 172430), sesame seeds (USDA FDC 170150), fried broad beans (USDA FDC 174285), yellow split peas (USDA FDC 172421), dried shrimp (USDA FDC 175180), raw tomato (USDA FDC 170457), lime juice (USDA FDC 167747), fish sauce (USDA FDC 173580), and vegetable oil (USDA FDC 171413). Proportions based on standard recipe documentation from peer-reviewed culinary literature on Burmese cuisine. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous fermented vegetable and mixed legume salad components; reference anchored to University of Sydney GI Database entries for legumes (boiled lentils GI ~29, chickpeas GI ~28, peanuts GI ~14) and fermented/pickled vegetables (GI ~32 range for mixed fermented vegetable dishes)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lahpet thoke in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 32 is estimated from the weighted contribution of the dish's primary glycemic components: fried legumes (broad beans, yellow split peas, peanuts) with GI values of 14–40 per University of Sydney GI Database, and fermented tea leaves which contribute minimal glycemic load. The overall dish GI is estimated at 32 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 21.0) / 100 = 6.7, using 200 g serving with 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, dried shrimp, and fermented tea leaves
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume tea leaf salad in moderation due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1040 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents a significant proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or dried shrimp can lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by fermented tea leaves, legumes, and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tea leaf salad has an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.7 per standard serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high fibre content (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g) and protein from legumes and dried shrimp further support postprandial glucose control, though sodium from fish sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame offset by elevated sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tea leaf salad provides heart-beneficial unsaturated fatty acids (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g combined mono- and polyunsaturated fat) and dietary fibre from legumes and fermented tea leaves, which may support lipid profiles; however, the high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g) poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing hypertension or heart disease, and portion control or sodium reduction modifications are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tea Leaf Salad, known in Burmese as Lahpet Thoke (လက်ဖက်သုတ်), is one of Myanmar's most iconic and culturally significant dishes. It is built around lahpet — fermented or pickled tea leaves — which are unique to Burmese culinary tradition and have been consumed in Myanmar for centuries, historically associated with ceremonial and social occasions. The dish is assembled rather than cooked: fermented tea leaves are placed at the centre of a plate and surrounded by an array of crunchy toppings including fried garlic, dry-roasted peanuts, fried sesame seeds, fried broad beans, fried yellow split peas, dried shrimp, fresh tomato slices, and green chillies. The salad is dressed with lime juice, fish sauce, and a small amount of oil, then tossed together at the table. The flavour profile is complex and layered — simultaneously sour from the fermented tea leaves and lime, savoury and umami-rich from the dried shrimp and fish sauce, nutty from the peanuts and sesame, and mildly bitter from the tea leaves themselves. The texture contrasts soft, slightly chewy fermented leaves with the crunch of fried legumes and nuts. Nutritionally, tea leaf salad is a moderately energy-dense dish (approximately 185 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g) from legumes and dried shrimp, and a favourable fat profile dominated by unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame. Dietary fibre content is notable at approximately 3.2 g per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is sodium, estimated at approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Tea leaf salad is served as a starter, side dish, or snack in Myanmar and in Burmese restaurants internationally, including in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for lahpet thoke as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component ingredient data using standard recipe proportions documented in peer-reviewed culinary literature on Burmese cuisine. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for the dish's primary glycemic components. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient entries; no single approved-source composite entry exists for lahpet thoke. Values should be updated if a direct approved-source measurement becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for the component ingredients of this dish and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed zero as the dish uses plant-based oils and no hydrogenated fats are present in the standard recipe. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for legumes and fermented vegetables; no direct GI measurement for lahpet thoke exists in the approved GI literature. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which are clearly erroneous for this dish and have been disregarded. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains dried shrimp and fish sauce which may not meet halal certification standards depending on sourcing; this should be verified per restaurant. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from peanut and sesame seed fat composition data in USDA FoodData Central. 8. Sodium estimate of 520 mg per 100 g reflects a standard restaurant preparation; home preparations with reduced fish sauce may have lower sodium.
